data.title: Teladoc Health: A Beaten-Down Bargain Worth A Second Look (Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Teladoc Health (TDOC) is not an easy investment to make. The appeal here is that this stock has been completely beaten down and is priced at just 7x next year's free cash flow.

However, the issue the business faces is that there's just too little topline growth. And without the growth in its engine to drive the business forward, tackling its debt profile is a problem.

So, I believe that there's enough here for me to upgrade this stock to tepid buy, having been neutral on it for the past few months. But I'm also rather clear about the risks.

Rapid Recap

As I discussed back in February 2025, Teladoc was a Deep Value Returns recommendation, where investors made 40%+ in 3 months, see below.

Looking back now, my timing looks fortuitous. Maybe. Probably. But does it matter?

I had a prepared mind going in. And recognized when the risk-reward had changed away from me and my subscribers. So, we sold. And kept neutral since.

Teladoc's Near-Term Prospects

Teladoc is the default virtual healthcare provider.

Whether it’s seeing a doctor online or managing chronic conditions, they’ve built a platform that makes care more accessible.

One of their biggest success stories is BetterHelp, which is now the largest direct-to-consumer virtual therapy platform in the world. Over a million people used it in 2024, and nearly half were brand new to therapy.

Plus, it’s fast (most people get matched with a therapist in under 48 hours), and it’s flexible—either paid directly or now increasingly covered by insurance, thanks to the recent acquisition of UpLift, which already works with health plans covering over 100 million lives. That opens the door for more people to get care.

That being said, Teladoc isn’t alone. In mental health, they’re up against other virtual care startups like Talkspace (TALK), as well as, traditional health systems ramping up their own virtual offerings. So, it's not like Teladoc is ever going to operate in a winner-takes-all sector.

Given this context, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Revenue Growth Rates Could Stabilize

The big question for investors is simple: Can Teladoc find sustainable growth again? Because if investors can’t get comfortable with Teladoc’s “return to growth” story, then—no matter how cheap the stock looks—it’ll likely keep getting cheaper.

There’s some reason for near-term optimism. Teladoc’s second half of 2025 is set up to show growth, largely because it’s coming off easy comps—H2 2024 saw two straight quarters of -3% y/y revenue. But that’s the low-hanging fruit.

What really matters is whether Teladoc can get back to something like 5% top-line growth in 2026. Without that, I suspect most investors will view the stock as too speculative—even if the valuation looks attractive.

TDOC Stock Valuation — 7x Forward Free Cash Flow

Teladoc is currently trading at just 7x forward free cash flow. On paper, that looks cheap. But there are a few caveats.

For starters, the company still has around $350 million of net debt. That’s not fatal—but it’s a blemish investors can’t ignore. Personally, I prefer companies with a net cash position of at least 5% of their market cap—it gives the thesis a second layer of margin of safety.

In Teladoc’s case, we’re not only facing uncertain growth prospects, but also a balance sheet that could quickly become a problem if anything goes wrong. Debt doesn’t matter—until suddenly, it does.

Furthermore, at the start of the year, Teladoc was guiding for up to $220 million of free cash flow.

But that changed.

In Q1 2025, Teladoc lowered its guidance to about $190 million. As a result, I now think $205–210 million in 2026 is probably the best investors can realistically hope for.

That brings us back to valuation.

The stock is priced at 7x forward free cash flow. This means that Teladoc trades at an adjusted PEG ratio of around 3.5x (7x FCF divided by ~2% expected revenue growth). That’s not screaming "cheap," even with the low multiple.

As a reference point, I'll consider businesses with a PEG ratio below 3x to be compelling. But I have made exceptions from time to time.

Risk Factors

The biggest risk? There’s no clear growth driver.

Yes, Teladoc has a strong narrative. Yes, there's a relative new CEO at Teladoc. And yes, Chuck Divita, the new CEO, argues that aggressive changes are being made. But as Warren Buffett once said:

When a management team with a reputation for brilliance tackles a business with a reputation for poor economics, it is the reputation of the business that remains intact.

The second risk is the debt. Until Teladoc meaningfully reduces its debt load, the market won’t reward it with a higher multiple.

That said, if Teladoc can generate around $200 million in free cash flow over the next 12 months and use that to pay down debt, the company could reach a net neutral balance sheet—where cash and debt cancel each other out. That would be a meaningful step forward, where I could see the stock getting re-rated higher.

The Bottom Line

I’m upgrading Teladoc from a hold to a tepid buy. Why? Because at 7x forward free cash flow, the stock is undeniably cheap on paper.

The setup for H2 2025 looks decent, thanks to easier comps, and if they can hit $200 million+ in free cash flow while chipping away at the debt, that changes the story.

Sure, the growth outlook is still murky, and the balance sheet isn’t exactly bulletproof.

But there’s just enough here—enough cash flow, enough potential debt progress, and enough strategic upside with BetterHelp and UpLift—that I think the risk/reward is starting to lean in investors' favor again.

I'm not pounding the table... but I’m definitely more interested again.

data.title: NMCO: Buying Opportunity Has Passed, CEF Gets Another Sell Rating
data.description:
Introduction

What the IRS tax brackets will look like after 2025 is still in the hands of Congress, with the GOP House and GOP Senate not on the same page for the final version of the “Big Beautiful Bill.” Today’s brackets revert back to 2016, inflation adjusted, if a new law isn’t passed. All of this matters for municipal bondholders as interest isn’t subject to federal taxes and one’s marginal tax bracket rate affects how they are evaluated against ed taxable fixed income assets, like corporate bonds.

I last reviewed the Nuveen Municipal Credit Opps Fund (NYSE:NMCO) in the fall of 2023 (article link), giving it a rare Strong Sell rating as interest rate hikes were destroying its NAV, and pulling the price down with. For reasons explained here later, while up, I’m still giving NMCO a Sell rating.

Nuveen Municipal Credit Opps Fund review

Seeking Alpha describes this CEF as:

The Fund’s primary investment objective is to provide a high level of current income exempt from regular U.S. federal income tax. The Fund’s secondary investment objective is to seek total return. Under normal circumstances, the Fund will invest at least 80% of its Assets in municipal securities, the income from which is exempt from regular U.S. federal income taxes. Benchmark: S&P Municipal Yield TR USD

This CEF has Common Assets of $594MM, with a Total Exposure of $1B thanks to its 42% leverage ratio. Nuveen provides fees at two levels.

Since NMCO uses leverage, performance is affected in two ways as rates move up and down as yield and leverage costs change.

Nuveen adds to the above description with this:

The Fund invests primarily in high yielding, low- to medium-quality municipal securities that, at the time of investment, are rated Baa/BBB or lower or, if unrated, are judged by the portfolio managers to be of comparable quality. No more than 30% of the Fund's managed assets will be in municipal securities rated CCC+/Caa1 or lower at the time of investment or unrated but judged to be of comparable quality. No more than 10% of the Fund's managed assets can be in defaulted securities or securities of issuers in bankruptcy or insolvency proceedings at the time of investment.

Source: nuveen NMCO.

The Fund is scheduled to terminate at the close of business on 10/1/2031. That said, the Fund’s Board of Trustees, without any action by the shareholders of the fund, can extend the Fund’s term for up to two one-year periods.

Holdings review

One thing I like about Nuveen is they provide more data than most funds do about the fund's portfolio.

Even excluding zero-coupon bonds, the average bond price of $85 leaves a lot of upsides if bonds are upgraded or interest rates decline. for those worried about the AMT process, 20% of the income from NMCO is exempt from those calculations. Starting with sectors, we see that almost 32% comes from the two tax-oriented sectors, with nice diversification otherwise.

With 63% of the bonds not rated, it limits the value of the next table. While most funds of this type usually have a high percentage not rated, over 60% stands out.

That said, with over 40% of the rated bonds being below investment-grade, the state of the economy comes into play more so than a fund invested mostly in investment-grade bonds, especially with over 7% rated B or lower where defaults become way too common.

Next, we see the fund is well diversified across the states, keeping in mind the percentages reflect where the issues is located, not that the state itself was the issuer.

Next we see that only a small percent of the portfolio matures over the next four years, though 90% of the portfolio is eligible to be Called over that time period. The above data table shows a long effective duration of 16+ years, with the WAM being 21 years, typical of municipal bond funds.

With the current estimated yield on BB-rated bonds, the low maturity rate is a positive as I estimate the majority of the coupons are over 5.5% of those maturing soon.

Top holdings

When compiling the data by Obligator, we see Puerto Rico is the biggest one, with the Top 10 accounting for 27% of the portfolio.

Distributions review

Last June, Nuveen did an across-the-board distribution hike on many of their CEFs with the hope the higher payout would narrow the discount the funds were experiencing. If that happened, it will be shown later. What it did do was change the sources needed to maintain the higher payout, which now is only covered by income at a 63% rate recently.

Over the past fiscal year, ROC provided 31% of the money paid to investors, with the most recent payment requiring 19% ROC.

Price and NAV review

After its post COVID recovery, NMCO has not done very well, though it started too when the FOMC started cutting rates and that would be expected. The next chart shows how the discount (or premium) has moved since inception.

The current 2% discount is less than normal, and this is reflected in that NMCO had an average discount of 5% over the past five years. As for the payout bump-up closing the discount, like other Nuveen CEFs, it did into the fall only to widen again after that.

CEF comparison

Here I included the other Nuveen CEFs also labeled as National High Yield.

  • Nuveen Municipal High Income Opportunity Fund (NMZ)
  • Nuveen Municipal Income (NMI).

NMCO has a consistent history of underperforming compared to these other similar funds, with it being the best only at the 5-year interval. Even worst, those poor results also inflicted investors with the highest StdDev! Based on the small discount currently available and its poor relative returns, I gave NMCO a Sell rating, though I thought about leaving it as a Strong Sell still.

Portfolio strategy

This importance of marginal tax rates hit home this year when filing my dad’s Trust under its EIN, not my dad’s SSN for the first time. Besides losing the joint table benefits, the Trust rate of 22% jumped to 37% and at a much lower level. While it was my mistake, it would have been expected that his CPA would have warned me. One result is I upped his municipal bond exposure to 17% of the portfolio. I’m preparing an article outlining the other changes I made, moving the overall strategy away from income generation to more growth oriented. With the account having a large capital loss carry forward, any trade would be “tax free.”

The main point is that one needs to be diligent to not only changes in the income tax laws, both federal and their state’s, but how a change in filing status might require a strategy change to improve the account’s after-tax results.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Headline Roulette Is A Game: Equity Investing Is Not
data.description:
By Nelson Yu

Transcript

Don’t play headline roulette. Those headlines have been whiplashing us back and forth between positive and negative sentiment. While it’s impossible to ignore these headlines, it’s important for a long-term investor to really think about what matters.

What really matters is understanding, well, where are the sources of underlying company growth? It’s never a good idea to sell at a moment when your emotions are high and you’re panicking. If you sold in April, you would’ve missed out on a 20% rally since that time. We started off the year with this consensus view of US exceptionalism. Nothing was going to stop the US equity market. It was the land for technology. It was going to be protected from any tariff announcement, and the markets were just going to go up. Small-caps were on a tear. You come to today, and now the question is, is US exceptionalism dead? And that was really driven by just some weakness in the dollar and international equity markets outperforming the US equity markets.

While we shouldn’t be driven by conventional wisdom, we need to engage with it. The headlines do tell us about important risks that we need to focus on. Recently, the escalation of risks in the Middle East reminds us how important geopolitical risks can be. The real challenge that investors face in terms of their overall wealth creation is the fact that growth is getting scarce. You’ve got inflation and you’ve got a concentration in the equity markets. So in order to invest for the long term, you need to think about what are the longer-term opportunities I have. You have a massive revolution in productivity that could happen because of AI. There are many beneficiaries that haven’t shown up yet. You’ve got an energy transformation that still needs to happen to power that AI. And finally, with deglobalization, we need to reinvest in our supply chain and our manufacturing onshore. There’s so many investment opportunities that can happen over the long term that investors can profit from.

Have a disciplined strategy, stick to it. We need to find companies with the strategy to win, whether that’s looking across competitive moats, or if your question is on US exceptionalism. There’s plenty of US companies with the right to win. However, you also have to think about Europe, Japan [and] emerging markets. There are also plenty of companies out there that can generate very high returns. And as a matter of fact, you can use deglobalization as your ally by reducing volatility.

And finally, defensive equity strategies [are] so important in this environment. Companies with stable earnings streams, thinking about the ups and downs and how that can affect your portfolio is a really important part of your long-term strategy. If investors just keep following headlines, they won’t be able to access these critical areas of growth that will generate return opportunities for the future.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams, and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fidus Investment: High Yield, Special Dividends And NAV Upside
data.description:
Business development company Fidus Investment Corporation (NASDAQ:FDUS) is selling for a moderate 5% premium to net asset value while covering its base dividend with net investment income and growing its net originations.

The business development company is primarily oriented toward the origination of First Liens and has profited from a seismic shift toward higher quality loans in the last couple of years. Fidus Investment's gross and net origination trends are bullish and indicate potential for organic net investment income growth.

My last stock classification on Fidus Investment was 'Strong Buy' as I viewed the business development company's dividend as being well-covered by net investment income.

In the last quarter, Fidus Investment paid a $0.11 per share extra dividend which supplemented the company's $0.43 per share per quarter base dividend.

Fidus Investment has special dividend potential, thanks to its excess coverage, and I think that the BDC could re-rate into a higher NAV multiple moving forward.

High-Grading Portfolio And Growing Originations Underline Core Investment Proposition

Fidus Investment is a floating-rate business development company with a focus on First Lien origination in the U.S. middle market segment.

As of March 31, 2025, Fidus Investment had an investment portfolio valued at $1.15 billion, up 6% QoQ, thanks to robust demand for new loans. The debt portfolio consisted overwhelmingly of First Liens with a remainder spread out over other investments, including Second Liens, Subordinated Debt and Equity.

Fidus Investment has instituted a significant change in the structure of its debt investment portfolio in recent years by accentuating new originations in First Lien debt instead of Second Lien debt. As a consequence, investors have enjoyed a substantial high-grading in portfolio quality for the business development company, particularly since 2020. As of today, about 79% of Fidus Investment's debt is high-quality First Lien debt.

Fidus Investment's net origination improved considerably in the March-quarter, thanks to high gross originations, but also because of a level of loan repayments that was less than half of what the business development company received in the prior quarter. In the last four quarters, Fidus Investment's profited from $22.6 million in average net originations which compares to $58.3 million in 1Q25.

Thanks to higher net originations (which were positive in five of the last six quarters), Fidus Investment's net investment income rose 3% YoY to $18.2 million. Many BDCs reported falling net investment income in recent quarters, as well sliding portfolio values, as portfolio companies accelerated their loan repayments in a high-rate environment.

Fidus Investment's net investment income easily covers the present dividend and with net originations being positive, we should be able to see growing NII in the next couple of quarters as well.

Fidus Investment's credit quality is also looking very compelling, with the BDC's non-accrual ratio of 0.8% clocking in below the BDC peer group average of 1.1% as of March 31, 2025.

Non-accruals are an important KPI to watch as an increase in non-accruals almost always foreshadows increasing NAV risks (due to BDCs being forced to write down the carrying value of their loans) and higher dividend risks as well.

Well-Covered Base Dividend, Excess Coverage Drives Special Dividend Potential

Robust and consistent excess dividend coverage over time is of utmost importance when it comes to choosing business development companies.

Fidus Investment offers this: The BDC earned $0.53 per share in net investment income in 1Q25 which compares to a total dividend pay-out of $0.54 per share (reflecting a pay-out ratio of 102%).

Just with regard to the base dividend of $0.43 per share, however, the dividend pay-out ratio was just 81% and thus on about the same level as the LTM dividend pay-out ratio. Low variability in the LTM dividend pay-out ratio compared to a BDC' quarterly dividend pay-out ratios indicate a high level of net investment income quality.

Moderate 5% Premium To NAV

Fidus Investment is selling for a moderate premium of 5% to net asset value as of the time of writing this article. This 5% premium is based on the company's last reported net asset value of $19.39 per share as of March 31, 2025 and might undervalue the BDC's solid 80%, particularly because the effective dividend yield could be much higher.

In 1Q25, Fidus Investment paid a total of $0.54 in dividends which included the $0.43 per share base dividend per quarter plus an extra dividend of $0.11 per share to distribute portfolio income exceeding the BDC's net investment income.

The implied net asset value premium places Fidus Investment only slightly above the BDC peer average Price-To-NAV ratio of 1.03x. In my view, Fidus Investment could sell for 1.10x net asset value as the base dividend is well-covered by NII and the BDC has a non-accrual ratio comparable to that of BDC champion Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC). Thus, an intrinsic value around $21.32 per share could be deduced vs. a present stock price of $20.36.

The two most expensive business developing companies are Ares Capital and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BSXL) which are selling at 11% to 12% premiums to their respective net asset values. FS KKR Capital Corp. (FSK) has an above average non-accrual ratio of 2.1% and therefore has below-average credit quality in its debt investment portfolio. This is the reason why the BDC is selling for the lowest Price-TO-NAV ratio of 0.9x, reflecting a 10% discount from the last reported net asset value.

Potential Concerns About A Long Position In Fidus Investment

Fidus Investment is fundamentally a floating-rate business development company. As of March 31, 2025, Fidus Investment had 72.8% exposure to floating-rate loans in its debt investment portfolio, so if the central bank were to respond in the latter half of the year to moderating inflation by lowering interest rates, the business development company might see lower portfolio income and a diminishing margin of safety as far as its dividend is concerned.

My Takeaway

Fidus Investment covered its base dividend well with net investment income in 1Q25 and so far, the central bank has proven to be quite reluctant to lower interest rates, which works to the benefit of floating-rate business development companies. Passive income investors therefore continue to have a strong reason to own the floating-rate BDC to produce income.

Moreover, the BDC is growing its net originations which is going to add portfolio incrementally moving forward.

With the base dividend of $0.43 per share per quarter being well-covered by net investment income and the portfolio growing, I think that the present rate environment allows for upside in Fidus Investment's valuation multiple. Strong Buy.

data.title: Affordability Of U.S. New Homes Worsened In May 2025
data.description:
The United States' new home affordability crisis worsened slightly in May 2025, as a higher median new home sale price combined with an increase in the nation's average mortgage rate.

The negative development means the monthly mortgage payment for the median new home sold in the United States once again consumes more than 36% of the pre-tax income of a household earning the median household income. May 2025 represents the 38th consecutive month in which the typical new home sold in the U.S. has been beyond the affordable grasp of the typical American household.

Here are the numbers being that assessment for May 2025. The initial estimate of the median sale price of a new home sold in the U.S. during the month is $426,600. Meanwhile, the 30-year conventional fixed-rate mortgage carried an average interest rate of 6.82% during the month. For a household taking out a mortgage with a zero-down payment [1], the principal and interest portion of their mortgage payment would total $2,423.

That mortgage payment would consume about 40.1% of the monthly income earned by a household at the exact middle of the U.S. income distribution in May 2025. As such, it exceeds the upper threshold of affordability that lenders have historically used to determine whether they will loan money to a household that has no other debt. For households that do carry other debt, lenders prefer their monthly mortgage payments consume no more than 28% of their pre-tax household income. Mortgage borrowers have more financial resilience to deal with unexpected expenses when their mortgage payments fall below these affordability thresholds.

The latest update of our chart tracks the changing relative affordability of the typical new home sold in the U.S. is for the typical American household with respect to the mortgage lending industry's key affordability thresholds from January 2000 through May 2025.

Looking ahead to June 2025, the interest rate for the average 30-year conventional fixed-rate mortgage held steady at 6.82%, which means borrowing costs remain elevated. With mortgage rates stuck, for the affordability of new homes to improve, one or more of the following two things would need to happen:

  • A decline in the price of the median new home sold in the U.S.
  • A significant increase in the income earned by the median household.

About the New Home Affordability Crisis

The affordability crisis for new homes has its origin in the high inflation that was unleashed in March 2021. Although it rose slowly at first, the cost of monthly mortgage payment began to skyrocket after December 2021. As a percentage of median household income, the monthly mortgage payment for a new home climbed above the key 36% threshold of relative affordability in April 2022, remaining above it in every month since.

Notes

[1] We use the zero-down payment scenario because it provides a simple way to account for the opportunity costs of paying a down payment when buying a new home for many homebuyers. Meanwhile, there are several lending programs for qualified homebuyers that do provide a zero-down payment option to try to make buying a home more affordable.

References

U.S. Census Bureau. New Residential Sales Historical Data. Houses Sold. [Excel Spreadsheet]. Accessed 25 June 2025.

U.S. Census Bureau. New Residential Sales Historical Data. Median and Average Sale Price of Houses Sold. [Excel Spreadsheet]. Accessed 25 June 2025.

Freddie Mac. 30-Year Fixed-Rate Mortgages Since 1971. [Online Database]. Accessed 27 July 2025. Note: Starting from December 2022, the estimated monthly mortgage rate is taken as the average of weekly 30-year conventional mortgage rates recorded during the calendar month.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Huron Consulting: Digital Gains Vs. Acquisition Pains
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Over the past decade, Huron Consulting Group Inc. (HURN) has evolved into a leaner, more capital-efficient business with a strong foothold in healthcare and education. This is underpinned by growing digital and managed services that support stable cash flows and returns above its cost of capital.

Its improving margins and ROIC trends make for a solid foundation. However, its reliance on acquisitions, with historical goodwill write-downs, means execution risks remain. My valuation only provided a 14% margin of safety based on the current market price. As such, I would wait for a better entry price.

Background

Huron is a global professional services firm delivering consulting, managed services, and digital solutions.

The consulting and managed services cover strategy, cost optimization, people transformation, and revenue cycle outsourcing. These services are the biggest revenue contributors, accounting for an average of 58% of total revenue over the past three years.

The balance of revenue came from digital solutions. Huron uses tech and data to deliver software, automation, analytics, EHR/ERP, and proprietary platforms.

Regarding customer profile, about 50% of the past 3 years' revenue came from the Healthcare segment. Another 32% came from the Education segment, with the remainder from the Commercial segment.

Although the company operates internationally, Huron is a US-centric business, as “financial results of our international operations are not significant.”

Competitive Position

I see the company as having a strong brand in healthcare and education consulting, supported by long-standing client relationships. Its numerous cultural awards (Vault, Forbes, Glassdoor) also help attract top consulting talent. This is critical in this people-driven business.

A decade ago, Huron operated primarily as a traditional consulting and coaching firm. Starting around 2018, Huron increasingly emphasized digital transformation as a core part of its competitive advantage. This was pivotal in driving revenue growth over the past few years as it enabled Huron to expanded its addressable market and moved towards more recurring, higher-margin services.

Huron has also used acquisitions to deepen its expertise in core verticals. In healthcare and education, acquisitions have enabled Huron to build out capabilities in areas like revenue cycle management, and higher education fundraising and governance. At the same time, select acquisitions have bolstered Huron’s technology and analytics portfolio.

Collectively, these have reinforced Huron’s position as an integrated consulting, managed services, and digital partner, enhancing its competitive moat.

Acquisition-Led Growth

Over the past decade, revenue grew at 8.7% CAGR, driven by both organic growth and acquisitions. The company did not breakdown its revenue growth into these 2 components. But you can get a sense of this by comparing the amount spent on CAPEX vs acquisitions.

From 2015 to 2024, Huron incurred $475 million on net acquisitions compared to $217 million on net CAPEX. This back-of-the-envelope analysis suggests that about 2/3 of the growth came from acquisitions.

With hindsight, you should not be surprised by the acquisition strategy. Huron primarily operates in the US management consulting market with deep specialization in healthcare and higher education. These are mostly low single-digit growth sectors as exemplified by the following market research reports:

“Management consulting in the US…revenue to grow an annualized 3.5% to an estimated $404.1 billion over the past five years, including an estimated 1.2% growth rate in 2025 alone.” Ibis World

“…the US Education Consulting Market Size was estimated at 703.02 (USD Million) in 2023…CAGR (growth rate) is expected to be around 4.753% during the forecast period (2025 to 2023).” Market Research Future

“The global healthcare consulting services market size was estimated at USD 33,597.6 million in 2024…growing at a CAGR of 8.3% from 2025 to 2030... North America was the largest revenue-generating market in 2024.” Grand View Research

While acquisition played a big role in revenue growth, it was not plain sailing all the time. In 2017 and 2020, the company incurred large goodwill impairments that changed the operating profits for the year into losses before tax.

The 2017 write-down was very substantial, equivalent to 22% of the 2016 total assets. The 2020 write-down was a smaller one amounting to about 5% of the 2019 total assets.

While management described these write-downs as the result of updated cash flow projections and market-based valuations, one could reasonably interpret this as ‘management-speak’ for having overpaid on some past acquisitions.

Why Profits Grew Slower Than Revenue

PAT growth did not fully keep pace with revenue growth. From 2015 to 2024, PAT grew at a 7.9% CAGR. As illustrated in Chart 1, this was due to a reduction in the gross profit margin. We see a similar picture for the contribution margin. These declines only started to stabilize post-2020.

According to the company’s Form 10-K filings, the margin compression was largely driven by higher consultant compensation costs, lower utilization rates, a growing mix of fixed-fee engagements, and underperformance in certain practices. Post-2020, margins began to stabilize as Huron shifted towards a more integrated, technology-enabled model underpinned by disciplined cost management.

A positive development over the same period was the reduction in the Selling, General and Administrative (SGA) margin from 22% in 2015 to 19% in 2024. More significantly, Huron improved its cost structure by reducing the proportion of fixed costs to total costs from 35% in 2015 to 22% in 2024.

This leaner cost structure means that as the company continues to scale its revenues, there will be a multiplier effect on profits, effectively offsetting some of the impact from lower gross profit margins.

The picture that emerged was that profit growth was due to higher revenue supported by better cost control. It was not due to margin expansion.

Note to Op Profit Profile. I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA, Depreciation & Amortization, R&D
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales - Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue – Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

There are strong indications that Huron’s current gross profit and contribution margins, and leaner cost structure are sustainable from a structural and strategic standpoint.

Over the past several years, Huron deliberately shifted its business model toward a more integrated mix of consulting, managed services, and digital solutions. This evolution not only diversified its revenue streams but also reduced dependence on traditional utilization-heavy consulting work, helping to stabilize margins.

Its lower fixed costs as a% of total costs came from streamlining its office footprint and building a more flexible global delivery model. While some risks remain around wage pressures, Huron’s pivot to higher-value digital and recurring services provides a structural buffer.

Improving Returns

I would point out that while the PAT growth rate was lower than the revenue growth rate, there as improving return trends over the past decade.

  • ROIC improved from 8.1% in 2015 to 15.9% in 2024, with an average of 7.7%.
  • ROE increased from 9.1% in 2015 to 20.8% in 2024, with an average of 20.8%

With the average ROIC and ROE higher than the current 6.9% WACC and 7.3% cost of equity, the company created shareholder value.

The improving returns were because the company became more capital efficient. For example, over the past decade, the total capital employed (total equity + debt – cash) only grew at 0.4% CAGR. The equity base also shrank from 2015 to 2024 due to its share buybacks.

The improving capital efficiency is reflected in the following metrics.

  • Gross profitability (gross profits/total assets) in 2024 was 38% higher than that in 2015.
  • Asset turnover went from 60% in 2015 to 111% in 2024.

Peer Comparison

To compare Huron’s performance, I consider the following publicly listed companies that offer management consulting and digital transformation services.

i) Accenture plc (ACN). The world’s leading professional services firm in digital transformation and cloud implementations, offering a useful benchmark for scale, technology partnerships, and profitability in complex, industry-specific consulting and managed services.

ii) CRA International (CRAI). A boutique management consulting and economic advisory firm with strong practices in healthcare analytics, antitrust, and life sciences, operating a high-margin, utilization-focused consulting model closely aligned with Huron’s capital-light approach.

iii) ExlService Holdings (EXLS). A data analytics and digital operations management company heavily embedded in the healthcare and insurance sectors, combining consulting-led transformation with recurring managed services, directly reflecting Huron’s evolving digital and outsourcing strategy.

iv) FTI Consulting (FCN). A diversified global consulting firm specializing in corporate finance, investigations, economic consulting, and health solutions, offering an asset-light, people-driven model similar to Huron’s but with a heavier focus on litigation support and restructuring.

v) Jacobs Solutions Inc. (J). Jacobs is a global leader in engineering, technical consulting, and infrastructure services, which overlaps with Huron through its majority-owned PA Consulting arm that delivers innovation, digital transformation, and strategic advisory services similar to Huron’s technology-enabled consulting and managed services. Refer to my SA article for more insights into Jacobs.

As can be seen from Table 1, Huron is among the small players in terms of the 2024 revenue, but ranked No. 3 in terms of revenue growth rate.

I consider the following metrics in the peer comparisons: return on capital, EBIT margin, unlevered free cash flow margin, and EPS. Refer to Charts 2 and 3.

i) Huron initially delivered one of the lowest returns on capital. But it began to improve in 2022, so that by 2024 it ranked No. 4. Huron doubled its return from 2015 to 2024. Considering that the peer leader's – Accenture's – return declined by about half over the past decade, I would consider Huron's performance acceptable.

ii) Huron ranked No. 2 in EBIT margin in 2015. But it then deteriorated to be in the bottom half of the peers before slowly improving from 2021, so that in 2024 it ranked No. 3. Note that among the peers, Huron was the only company whose 2024 EBIT margin was lower than that in 2015.

iii) Huron had the best free cash flow margin initially. But it declined so that in 2021, it was the worst performer. But it had since rebounded to rank No. 2 in 2024. In terms of the % of free cash flow margin, Huron’s 2024 margin was lower than that of 2015. Of the panel companies, there was only one other company whose 2025 margin was lower than its 2015 margin.

iv) Huron had the lowest quantum of EPS for most of the past decade. It also ranked No. 5 in terms of EPS growth rate over the past decade.

Compared to its peers, Huron’s performance over the past decade has been a story of gradual operational recovery and selective improvement, though not outright leadership.

Taken together, this simple peer analysis showed that Huron faced profitability and capital efficiency challenges mid-decade. But it had shown some improvements in recent years, albeit without reclaiming its earlier leadership for some metrics.

Financial Implications

I would rate Huron as moderately sound financially, as its following positive points were offset by the negative ones.

From 2015 to 2024, it delivered positive cash flow from operations every year. During this period, it had a very good cash conversion rate as it generated $1.2 billion cash flow from operations compared to the cumulative PAT of $276 million.

It had a good capital allocation plan as shown in Table 2. Its cash flow from operations was enough to cover its CAPEX and acquisitions.

Huron had several negative points financially.

As of the end of March 2025, it had $24 million in cash, equivalent to only about 2% of the total assets. It also had a debt-equity ratio of 125%. This is its past decade high.

But looking at Table 2, the cash and debt position is due to its significant share buybacks. These are within management control, and as such, I would not worry too much about these 2 metrics.

The other worrisome point was its 71% average Reinvestment rate (Reinvestment/NOPAT) over the past decade. I defined Reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisition – Depreciation & Amortization + increase in Net Working Capital.

But this high rate was due to its acquisitions. Excluding the acquisitions, the average Reinvestment rate dropped to 1%. This is exceptionally low as over the long term I would expect the Reinvestment rate to be governed by the fundamental growth equation of growth = Return X Reinvestment rate.

Valuation

My findings showed that Huron is a sound business with a decent moat and improving trajectory. It not only operates from a strong competitive base, but has structurally improved its capital efficiency. However, gross profit and contribution margins, while stable over the past few years were lower than those a decade ago.

It also generated consistent cash flows with returns well above its cost of capital. However, its reliance on acquisitions to drive growth, past goodwill impairments, and only moderate peer-relative performance temper the case for calling it a "wonderful company" in the Buffett sense.

As such, in valuing Huron, I adopted the following picture.

i) The base revenue would be the 2024 revenue with a 10% growth rate in Year 1. This growth rate would reduce proportionately to 4% in the terminal year (Year 6). Note that the 2024 growth rate was 9% and the Q1 2025 year-on-year growth rate was 11%. I assumed that in the terminal year, the company would rely only on organic growth.

ii) The base contribution margin would be the 2021 to 2024 average margin and remain unchanged over the valuation period.

iii) The base fixed cost margin would be the 2021 to 2024 average margin. This would improve proportionately so that in the terminal year it would be 15% lower. Note that the 2024 fixed cost margin was 16% lower than the 2021 margin.

iv) I also assumed that there would be annual restructuring charges of $10 million based on the past decade's average charges for mergers and restructuring charges. I have assumed that there would not be any asset write-downs.

v) The base capital turnover would be the 2021 to 2024 average value.

vi) The base Reinvestment margin would be the past decade's average rate. This rate would decrease (improve) so that in the terminal year, we would have a Reinvestment margin that would result in a Reinvestment rate given by the fundamental growth equation.

vii) The tax rate would be the past decade's average tax rate.

On such a basis, I obtained an intrinsic value of USD 156 per share compared to the market price of USD 137 per share (1 Jul 2025). There is only a 14% margin of safety compared to my 30% target.

Valuation Model

This was based on a multi-stage growth model as shown in Table 3, where the free cash flow to the firm [FCFF] was determined based on the following:

FCFF = EBIT(1 – t) - Reinvestment

EBIT = Revenue X Contribution margin – Fixed cost

Fixed cost = Revenue X Fixed cost margin

The $10 m annual cost for restructuring was modelled as an addition to the fixed cost derived from the fixed cost margin.

The Reinvestment rate was derived from the Reinvestment margin.

The other assumptions used are stated in the accompanying notes.

Notes to Table 3:

a. Proportionate reduction

b. Pegged to the growth rate

c. Assumed no improvement

d. Revenue X fixed cost margin, plus $10m per year for restructuring charges.

e. Revenue X Margin, and after accounting for Fixed costs

f. Assumed proportionate change

g. b X f

h. FCFF for each year = e - g

j. Refer to the WACC table

k. NPV for each year = (h X j)

l. Terminal for the year discounted at the terminal growth rate

m. 5 years NPV + terminal value

n. Inclusive of any excess TCE. Non-operating assets, MI, and Debt

o. Based on the number of diluted shares

The WACC was based on the first page results of a Google search for “Huron Consulting WACC” and is summarized in Table 4.

Risks And Limitations

My valuation assumed that Huron would be able to continue with its cost improvement trends. Despite this, my assumptions are a bit on the conservative side when I compare the projected values with the historical performance.

  • Its average revenue growth over the valuation period is 7.0% CAGR compared to its past decade's average revenue growth rate of 8.7% CAGR.
  • Its 12% ROIC in the terminal year is lower than its 2024 ROIC of 16%.
  • Its 12% terminal EBIT margin is slightly below its 2024 EBIT margin of 13%.

Given the above, you may think that there is some potential upside. But you have to take into account the following:

Over the past decade, the company needed to reinvest 71% of NOPAT to sustain growth. My valuation assumes this improves to 34% by the terminal year. Without this improvement, intrinsic value drops sharply.

I also assumed that there would not be any more asset write-downs. Over the past decade, the asset write-down was equal to about $31 million per year. With such a quantum of write-down, there would not be any margin of safety.

Huron has stated that it would continue to acquire companies as part of its acquisition plan. To improve its Reinvestment rate while continuing to acquire companies, the company must be able to acquire companies in a more cost-effective manner. Alternatively, Huron has to look for greater synergies when acquiring companies.

I don’t think the acquisition track record has been good, as illustrated below.

Over the past decade, the company spent about $475 million on acquisitions. From 2015 to 2024, the company’s PAT increased from $59 million to $117 million, equal to a $58 million increase.

I have shown earlier that acquisition accounted for about 2/3 of the revenue growth. Assuming proportionate profit contribution, I would deduce that the acquisition accounted for about $38 million of the profit increase.

This meant a 13-year payback period for the acquisition, assuming $38 million profit each year. This is, of course, a very simple back-of-envelope analysis as it ignored revenue and/or earnings timing. But I hope it gives you a sense that there are acquisition challenges.

As such, while my assumptions are conservative, I would want a 30% margin of safety before entering.

Conclusion

Huron is a fundamentally sound company with a well-defined niche in healthcare and education consulting, bolstered by its digital and managed services strategy.

Its stronger capital efficiency, and returns comfortably above its cost of capital underline a credible transformation story. However, the company’s historical reliance on acquisitions to drive growth, coupled with mixed results evidenced by goodwill impairments and modest acquisition paybacks, tempers the investment case.

While I would rate the company as fundamentally sound, there were 2 areas that I wished were better.

  • While its gross profit margin and contribution margin were stable over the past few years, they were lower than those a decade ago.
  • I rated the company as moderately sound financially.

At the same time, my valuation only provided a 14% margin of safety. As such, while I see Huron as a sound business on a better trajectory, I would wait for at least a 30% margin of safety before buying.

data.title: All The World Is A Bubble: Diversify Or Be Devoured
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

According to a study conducted by Comparison Advisor, 47% of total respondents said they manage their investments on their own. The study found that only 9% of American investors use a human financial advisor. If we break down the results by age of the investor, it was found that for people in their 30s, ~42% opted for a robo-advisor, while only 3% chose a human financial professional. Looking at people in their 50s, it was observed that ~11% use a financial advisor to manage their investments, while the number was 21% for people aged 60 and over. Since you are reading this article on Seeking Alpha, one of the largest ecosystems to cater to individual investors, it is fair to assume you manage your money (at least a healthy chunk of it).

When managing your own money, it is crucial to consider certain key factors. Billionaire investor Charlie Munger said diversification is for idiots who don’t know what they are doing.

Diversification is for those who don't know anything. Warren (Warren Buffett) calls them know-nothing investors. If you are capable of figuring out something that will work better, you're just hurting yourself looking for 50 (stocks) when three will suffice. Hell, one will suffice if you do it right. – Charlie Munger.

I am a big fan of Mr. Munger; he has been a terrific inspiration for many, and his wise words and insights have formed volumes covered in business school curricula. But I humbly believe he was wrong on this specific advice for retail investors.

Or maybe he was right, when we put high emphasis on the last few words of his sentence, “if you do it right.”

Can you do it right?

You can’t avoid catastrophic losses

We define a catastrophic loss as a permanent decline of more than 70% of the value from its peak price. According to a JPMorgan (JPM) study on Russell 3000 companies between 1980 and 2014, ~40% of stocks suffered catastrophic losses, with the number being higher in sectors such as technology, biotech, and metals and mining.

Moreover, since 1980, over 320 companies have been removed from the S&P 500 due to business distress, indicating significant turnover in the “passive” index. The study focused on companies that failed outright, or were removed due to substantial declines in their market value, or were acquired after experiencing such a decline. Note the surge in the number of companies removed from the index following the DOT-COM bust and the Great Financial Crisis, indicating that the index isn’t as proactive as one may have hoped.

Most Stocks Underperform

The JPM study also found that two-thirds of all individual stocks underperformed the Russell 3000 Index, with 40% of them posting negative absolute returns, underscoring the significant risk associated with investing in individual stocks. Of course, there were extreme winners, those that surged over 400%, and those that everyone is hunting. This represents the right tail of the chart, and constitutes ~7% of the universe.

Who Would Intentionally Buy A Bad Business?

No one knowingly buys a bad company. If investors know that something would constitute a catastrophic loss, they would know better than to invest in it. Retail investors often exhibit certain biases that make them comfortable holding individual stocks, making them vulnerable to poor investment decisions. We wrote an article in January 2024 that covered seven different types of bias, so we will not repeat those here. Instead, we will discuss two that specifically relate to the topic at hand.

Familiarity Bias: As the name suggests, people tend to favor things that are familiar to them, while showing less interest in things that are unfamiliar. There is irony here because we are told to invest in what we know and understand. But investing isn’t a full-time job for most individual investors. Individuals don’t have the time to understand the pros and cons of every possible investment. As a result, people tend to simplify things by gravitating towards what’s familiar.

Many investors believe that they fully understand a company and all the risks it faces. In reality, such certainty is difficult – if not impossible – to claim. The most relatable example is that of B. Riley Financial (RILY), where CEO was extremely confident with the investment in the Franchise Group, and believed he knew former FRG CEO Brian R. Kahn very well, and was confident to take a disproportionate stake that proved very costly for his reputation, shareholders, and employees.

There are many more examples, but another notable one is Pershing Square's nightmarish 8.5% stake in Valeant, because Bill Ackman thought it was a massive FCF (Free Cash Flow) machine, and publicly admired the brilliance of then-CEO J. Michael Pearson. The company then faced a host of challenges, including management mishaps, poor earnings, and guidance cuts, as well as questionable conference calls, which led to technical defaults on its debt. Pershing Square later sold its entire position, incurring a $3 billion loss.

The Illusion of control bias is the tendency for people to overestimate their ability to control events that are inherently uncertain. This bias can lead investors, analysts, and portfolio managers to believe that they have more control over the outcome of their analysis or investments than they actually do. Investors tend to think that they can hand-pick stocks to beat the market, spend time researching, analyzing financial statements and charts, and allow themselves to believe they have control over the outcome of their analysis. Investors also routinely try to time the market because they think they have insight into the direction of the markets and know when the markets will rise or fall. Traders, too, may fall into this trap, believing that frequent activity and quick trades equate to control and skill, when in fact, much of their short-term success may be driven by luck.

But Bad Businesses are Bad From The Get-Go, Right?

Not necessarily. While some businesses are indeed flawed from the outset, born with weak business models, poor leadership, or no real path to sustained profitability, many thriving companies turn bad over time due to strategic missteps, market shifts, regulatory pressure, poor use of financial engineering, and management complacency.

Businesses don’t always fail because we have gaps in our understanding or thesis. In fact, there are several factors that are completely outside the control of any investor, retail or institutional. A few examples include:

  • Commodity price risks that can’t be hedged away.

  • Changes in government policy through deregulation or re-regulation.

  • Trade policy shifts.

  • Foreign policy changes.

  • Fraud by company employees.

No matter how well you understand a company, elements like the above cannot be foreseen and controlled.

It also isn’t the case that catastrophic losses are only suffered by small companies, or those from a specific sector, or those that have weak profitability, overvaluation, or have massive leverage. Companies of different sizes have experienced such irrecoverable losses. Companies across various sectors, including profitable ones, and even those with strong balance sheets and modest valuations, experience such catastrophic losses.

What If I Only Buy Stocks That Go Up?

You may be surprised to learn that there is actually a greater risk of losses when buying a company that has experienced an exceptional price performance. Retail investors tend to place undue emphasis on ratings and recommendations from Wall Street analysts, when history shows that 80% of stocks that suffer catastrophic losses were rated STRONG BUY. Moreover, around half of these stocks had consensus price targets above their peak prices.

No company is immune to failure. The factors behind such declines are often unpredictable, reinforcing the need for diversification.

The Only Free Lunch in Investing

Diversification isn’t just about spreading your money around. It’s about protecting your portfolio from the unknowable, the unpredictable, and the uncontrollable. There’s a reason diversification is often referred to as the only free lunch in investing. It’s one of the few strategies that can, for income investors, reduce risk without necessarily reducing returns. By owning a wide range of income-producing securities across industries, geographies, and asset types, you gain much-needed income resilience. You position yourself to combat economic storms, company-specific failures, and even your own misjudgments and biases.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we don’t pretend to attempt to predict the future – we prepare for it. That’s why our Income Method is built on a foundation of broad and intentional diversification. We follow our Rule of 42, holding at least 42 different securities to ensure no single holding contributes more than ~2–3% of total income. This allows us to focus on consistency over concentration, and stability over speculation.

In a world where even great companies stumble and headlines can rattle markets, diversification is the one tool every investor should keep sharp. You can’t eliminate the risk of catastrophic failure of a few of your holdings, but you can keep your income flowing when the inevitable happens.

That’s the only free lunch we’ll ever get – and it’s one worth showing up for!

data.title: My Biggest Moves Ever: Why I Just Flipped My Dividend Portfolio On Its Head
data.description:
Introduction

It's finally time to make good on some promises.

For the past few months, I have promised readers to give them a full overview of my dividend portfolio, which used to account for nearly 100% of my net worth.

As I rarely make big portfolio changes because of my ultra-long investment mindset, I do not often write these updates. Also, unlike many others, I do not keep track of a million factors like daily dividend income, annual dividend growth, or much more advanced metrics.

That's because I believe in simplicity.

For me, only two things really count; everything else is a bonus:

  • Owning the best companies I can think of.
  • Using my Big Picture research framework to improve the odds of buying assets in the right sectors and being on top of major trends that can make or break an investment strategy.

Essentially, I spend 90% of my time every day on these two bullet points.

With that said, I made more promises to readers. On top of owing people a full overview of my portfolio, I also owe them an explanation as to why I just sold some assets.

On June 29, I wrote an article titled "3 Fast-Growing Stocks Disrupting A Trillion-Dollar Industry." In that article, someone noted that I did not disclose a long position in Lockheed Martin (LMT), a company I have owned for many years and often called one of my favorite investments.

I replied that I had indeed sold Lockheed shares. However, it wasn't part of some twisted pump & dump scheme or a sudden loss of confidence in the bullish thesis I have maintained since 2021.

No, I just made the biggest changes to my portfolio ever (yes, really), as I finally pulled the trigger on a plan I had for many years, which is buying physical real estate.

For that, I needed some (read: a lot of) liquidity.

That's the short version.

The long version is much more complex, as it's all part of a new strategy of mine to combine investments in stocks and real estate to build an even more bulletproof framework for long-term wealth.

In this article, I'll give you all the details and a full overview of my portfolio and strategy, as well as my plans for the future.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let's get right to it!

On a side note, in this very lengthy article (sorry editors!), I decided to provide as much food for thought as possible. Even if you dislike my personal portfolio or couldn't care less about my real estate investment, there's still something worth reading for everyone.

I Finally Pulled The Trigger On Real Estate

As most of my readers may know, I have been to Albania twice this year.

Initially, I went to get to know the country, as my former boss, who is now one of my closest friends, has Albanian roots and told me a lot about the country.

Because I knew about its tax advantages and attractive investment opportunities, I decided to combine business and pleasure on my first trip.

The first trip was quite something, as I had to get used to what is still a second-world country. After having visited Southwest Florida's nicest cities in February and March and living in the Netherlands, adjusting wasn't easy in the first few days, as privileged as that may sound.

Albania has a very low average income, a lot of stray dogs, often very bad infrastructure in some regions, and a very high level of corruption. It also has one of the most powerful mafias in the world, which even the Italian Mafia is afraid of.

However, I quickly got used to it and booked a second trip the moment I got back, this time with the goal to dive much deeper into its economy, investment opportunities, and more. That's also why I went with someone who had decades of experience investing in real estate.

I also decided to go further south to Saranda (Sarandë), a city some like to call the "Capital of Summer." That's not an official name. I found it online and really liked it, as it is extremely fitting. If you have ever been, you may agree.

The town is very small with a total population of roughly 23,000 people as of 2023 (Wikipedia). Unlike cities like Vlora (Vlorë), it doesn't have a lot of industry. Essentially, it's a mini version of Southern California, as the city is wedged between the Adriatic Sea and the mountains, which provides for an extremely favorable supply situation for its real estate market. I added the terrain view to the Google Maps screenshot below.

It's also a hotspot on Albania's Riviera, as it is extremely close to Dhermi and Himarë, two places with some of the best beaches on the planet. On top of that, it's a short boat ride away from the Greek island of Corfu, a famous tourist destination that I have yet to visit.

Anyway, during the night of my first stay in Saranda, the city was hit by a widespread power outage, which caused me to fall behind on some important work and reminded me that it's still a country in recovery mode.

However, that's where the bad news ends.

Despite the issues like corruption and low wages I mentioned earlier, Albania is emerging as a bright spot and one of the safest countries in the world. Although many neighborhoods have very low incomes and houses that seem to be falling apart, you can feel safe everywhere. In fact, I felt safer in the poorest areas I visited than in any major Dutch or American cities.

After suffering from communism for almost five decades, one of the longest periods of all countries, Albania is now seeing a boom in business.

  • Albania is one of the most beautiful countries. It has world-class beaches, gorgeous mountains (i.e., the Albanian Alps), and fast access to nations like Greece.
  • The government is spending a lot of money on infrastructure. This includes the expansion of the Tirana International Airport, the addition of the Vlora Airport, and a network of fantastic new roads.
  • Albania's value proposition is almost unrivaled. It has the beauty of Greece at a much lower price tag, which is now attracting billions in foreign capital. This includes a $1.4 billion investment from Jared Kushner, President Trump's son-in-law.
  • The country has very low taxes. This includes a maximum income tax bracket of 23% (in my country, it's 50%), a dividend tax of just 8%, and a maximum capital gains tax of 15%. Also, in some cases, digital nomads pay no income taxes for a limited period. Please note that I am no tax advisor. There are many specific situations where other rates apply.
  • It is attracting some of the best architects in the world, who are transforming its coast into an even better destination for tourists. Examples include this fascinating project.
  • On a side note, the header picture of this article also shows Saranda.

For all of these reasons and more, I decided to use some of the contacts I had made, which include a major developer of luxury real estate.

Because I never owned real estate (I always rented) and always had the goal of owning at least one property before turning 30, I was interested in finding out more about these opportunities, not necessarily planning on buying something.

Well, things went much faster than I could have imagined, as the meeting with the developer was a success, and I was simply blown away by the opportunities in Albanian real estate.

It also helped a lot that an investor had just defaulted on an apartment development, which put it back on the market. The timing could not have been better, as I decided to buy it.

Unfortunately, I cannot show you the website and pictures of the developer, as some may accuse me of promoting these assets. It is also not extremely safe if I tell the entire Internet where people can find me in the future.

However, I am still going to give you as many details as possible, as it's a major investment decision that also impacts my future plans.

  • It's a luxury apartment of roughly 1,100 square feet.
  • It's part of a building that will be finished in the summer of 2026.
  • I will have unobstructed views of the Adriatic Sea and Corfu (the Saranda Bay), something that is extremely rare.
  • It's five minutes away from the beach and close to the city's best restaurants.

I am bringing all of this up because it's prime real estate with benefits other than simply having a great place to live. For example, because South Albania is booming, cities like Saranda are seeing huge surges in property prices, which come with limited new construction. What used to be run-down neighborhoods are now new developments with build qualities often better than in most Western nations, as Albanian construction is top tier.

Also, because of favorable geography, the new supply is limited, which is why the city is already reducing the number of building permits, causing prices to rise even further.

This also benefits the rental market.

While I am planning on using it as a primary residence, the developer is also the property manager who helps people manage rentals. If I suddenly were to decide I'm moving out again, I could (according to current conditions) get a cap rate of 8-9%, net of management fees.

To me, that was a big deal, as it substantially lowers my financial risks. If everything goes according to plan, I bought something in a fast-growing market with tremendous income potential.

With that said, because I do not own property in the Netherlands, I could not get a mortgage. Essentially, I am paying all-cash for the property, which has both pros and cons:

  • Pros: I will own my property outright, meaning no interest payments.
  • Cons: I had to reserve a lot of cash for the deal.

Another thing you need to know is that I am taking on this development with another partner. Until the development is finished, we have to pay in installments, all dependent on construction progress.

I structured the deal with my partner in a way that makes me responsible for all initial payments. In other words, instead of paying gradually, I am paying my part right at the start, which brings me to my portfolio.

A Big Haircut, As I Sold 6 Investments (That I Still Like A Lot)

Technically speaking, I could have structured my deal differently and funded the project gradually. According to my calculations, it could have prevented me from selling any stock.

However, I did not do that.

By paying my part upfront, I do not have to add any new capital to the project, which means I can fully focus on my dividend (growth) portfolio and other business endeavors. While I am still dealing with paperwork, I have now delegated everything to the developer.

The decision was also influenced by the quality of my articles. I understood that if nearly all of my capital was committed to this project over the next 12-ish months, I wouldn't be able to make any new significant dividend investments. Since I discuss these investments daily, I worried it would make my articles dull if I never talked about my own stock investment plans.

Hence, by selling stock to fund the program, I not only have a fully paid property (still under construction) but also the opportunity to re-shape my portfolio and immediately start adding new capital in the weeks and months ahead.

The deal also gives me a foot in the door, as I will soon be able to work on deals with Albanian banks and lend against my new asset. While I am NOT planning on executing any of these new deals over the next 1–2 years, it's a plan I have for the future, as it could also allow me to focus more on dividend growth if future properties can generate sufficient cash flow.

With regard to my portfolio, here's what I changed:

  • I sold six investments.
  • I decided to sell most of my smallest holdings and sell entire positions instead of making some of my biggest investments smaller.
  • I now own 16 individual stocks, making my portfolio extremely concentrated.

Now, before I tell you which stocks I sold, I need to mention that I am still as bullish as I was on the day I bought these investments. I merely sold them to increase my portfolio's concentration, as selling a bit of everything would have some investments too small for my taste.

  • Lockheed Martin (LMT): Lockheed was not one of my smallest investments. However, I sold it simply to improve diversification. As none of my defense & aerospace investments were among my smallest holdings, I would have ended up owning five stocks in this industry in a portfolio of just 16 stocks. That's the sole reason behind the decision to sell. It hurt quite a bit selling LMT, as I believe it is way undervalued, with the potential to return >14% per year going forward. It also yields close to 3.0%.

  • Extra Space Storage (EXR): I consider EXR the best self-storage stock on the market. However, it was one of my smallest investments and a company that does not have a wide moat. As much as I love EXR (I would buy it back in a heartbeat), I think my Albanian real estate investment is a better use of my cash.
  • PepsiCo (PEP): Selling PepsiCo hurt, as I believe it is way too cheap after selling off more than 30% from its all-time high. However, because of this decline, it had become one of my smallest investments. Also, in light of my age, I believe investing in faster-growing companies may be the way to go, which eases the pain of selling this company so cheaply.

  • AbbVie (ABBV): A great company and one of the best blends of income and growth. However, it didn't make the cut due to its size. I will buy it back once I become more dependent on generating income from my investments.
  • Deere (DE) & Caterpillar (CAT): Selling these two caused me a bad night of sleep. These two were among my best performers, as I had bought them dirt-cheap during the pandemic. However, because they have done so well from the start, I never added aggressively, which is why they were still among my smallest positions. As I already own a ton of cyclical exposure, I sold them. It hurt, but it had to be done.

  • Danaher (DHR): Same story. DHR was one of my favorite stocks that simply didn't make the cut due to its size. While I still believe buying my property in Saranda is a better investment, I believe Danaher has what it takes to deliver returns way north of 14-15% in the years ahead, as the global healthcare supply market is bottoming.

After having sold these stocks, I had enough cash to fund my assets, which means it does not matter what happens to the stock market or global interest rates; I completely de-risked that investment.

Now, I own 16 ultra-high-conviction investments that form the backbone of my long-term wealth generation strategy, in addition to a hard asset that will hopefully be finished in late summer 2026.

My Ultra-High Conviction 16-Stock Dividend Growth Portfolio & Future Plans

The good news is that despite selling stocks, I still have a substantial six-figure portfolio, which allows me to retire in Albania without having to buy risky stocks with elevated yields.

However, I am just mentioning that to make clear that my portfolio is still serious business instead of some leftovers after having sold some stocks. I have no intentions of ever retiring. And that's not why I'm going to Albania.

Given my financial situation, I can start buying new investments in July/August again. If everything goes right, I will have more capital in stocks in 12 months than I did before I invested in Albania.

Essentially, if I do it right, I can soon start building wealth through both my financial assets (dividend stocks) and new property deals, as I would love to add rentals to my portfolio.

That's also why I do not mind that the average dividend yield of my portfolio has dropped to just 1.5% (excluding special dividends). This puts my yield 30 basis points ahead of the S&P 500 yield of 1.2% but way below the 4% yield of the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

That said, I will definitely not shift my focus away from dividend stocks to other investments. On Seeking Alpha, it's business as usual, which means I will continue sharing both lower-yielding dividend growth opportunities and high-yield income opportunities for a wide range of readers.

Now, here's my portfolio:

Name (Ticker) Sector/Industry Weighting Yield*
LandBridge (LB) Energy (Landowner - mineral rights, water rights, and surface rights) 14.8% 0.7%
Texas Pacific Land (TPL) Energy (Landowner - mineral rights, water rights, and surface rights) 14.2% 0.7%
Comfort Systems USA (FIX) Construction & engineering services 10.5% 0.3%
GE Aerospace (GE) Commercial and defense engine producer 6.5% 0.6%
Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL) Less-than-truckload transportation. 6.3% 0.7%
Union Pacific (UNP) Class 1 railroad 6.1% 2.3%
RTX Corp. (RTX) Major defense contractor 5.7% 1.9%
Antero Midstream (AM) Midstream energy (focus on gathering and processing) 5.7% 4.8%
CME Group (CME) Exchanges owner/operator 4.8% 1.8%
Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR) Industrial real estate in Southern California 4.1% 4.8%
L3Harris Technologies (LHX) Major defense contractor 4.0% 1.9%
Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) Canadian oil and gas producer. 4.0% 5.5%
Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP) Class 1 railroad 3.6% 0.8%
Carlisle Companies (CSL) Building products 3.6% 1.1%
Northrop Grumman (NOC) Major defense contractor 3.4% 1.9%
The Home Depot (HD) DIY home improvement and PRO sales 2.7% 2.5%

* = Excluding special dividends

As you can see, I am now massively overweight in my highest-conviction ideas, even more so than before. Also, selling some of my higher-yielding stocks has pushed my dividend yield to just 1.5%, as I already briefly mentioned.

None of my five biggest stocks pays a dividend of more than 1.0%, which is why a relatively small sector like real estate accounts for 13% of my entire dividend income, despite Rexford having a weighting of just 4.1% in my portfolio.

While I will be buying higher-yielding stocks, I do not have to focus on income anymore, as my move to Albania will free me of the wealth tax I am currently paying. This means I can afford to incorporate more lower-yielding stocks in my portfolio.

However, I will continue to provide well-balanced research. This does not mean I will focus more on lower-yielding stocks. On the contrary, I promised I would provide more content for retired investors, which is a promise I want to honor.

These changes also mean that my portfolio is even more concentrated in industrial and energy investments, which now account for almost 90% of my total exposure. The combined weighting in the S&P 500 is 12.0%.

Nonetheless, I am not crazy. As I have written in prior articles, both these sectors are highly diversified, as a big part of my industrial exposure consists of anti-cyclical defense companies, while most of my energy exposure is composed of ultra-high margin landowners and midstream without direct pricing risks. This is very different from a portfolio filled with oil and gas producers and cyclical industrial machinery companies.

That's why the industry breakdown makes me look a lot saner:

With regard to my performance, I am currently up 9.6% year-to-date, which is good but not very important to me, as I don't care to beat the market every year. I mainly care for the multi-year performance, as it keeps me from making short-term decisions to beat the market, if you know what I mean.

So, all things considered, on Seeking Alpha, it will remain business as usual.

For my personal financial future, I can start investing new capital at the end of July. I will focus a bit more on growth due to a more favorable tax situation, while also working on a way to improve my portfolio's income at some point down the road.

My first goal was to put myself in a spot where I can retire if needed. I am roughly 1 year away from being able to afford a very comfortable retirement in Albania. Even today, I could pull the trigger if I had to. However, as I already said, I am NOT retiring. It's just good to have a safety net.

Looking forward, my long-term plan is to build both my dividend portfolio and get into hard assets like rentals and bigger projects if opportunities present themselves.

Needless to say, I will keep readers up to date on anything I do!

Takeaway

I just made the biggest portfolio changes of my life, selling six high-quality stocks to fund my first real estate investment in Albania.

It wasn't an easy decision, as selling some of my favorite stocks really hurt, but it aligns with my long-term strategy, which is combining dividend growth stocks with hard assets for better wealth growth and preservation.

This move is also expected to improve my fiscal situation and open up new investment opportunities down the road.

Now, my portfolio is ultra-concentrated in just 16 high-conviction stocks, while my new Albanian apartment offers prime appreciation and rental potential.

All things considered, to me, simplicity wins. Whether it's stocks or real estate, I invest in the best opportunities I can find. I then try to hold them forever. And while diversification matters, conviction matters more to me.

Needless to say, I will diversify my portfolio a lot more going forward, using some of the stocks I frequently discuss on Seeking Alpha.

Having said all of this, thank you for reading all >3,600 words and for having an interest in my journey.

Stay tuned for more!

data.title: Bombardier: Monster Order Leads To A Price Target Upgrade
data.description:
Company Overview & Recent Results

Bombardier Inc. (TSX:BBD.B:CA) (OTCQX:BDRBF) is a leader in the aerospace industry and is headquartered in Montreal, Quebec. The company is known for manufacturing business jets and has a few key aircraft lines, with the Global, Challenger, and Learjet series. BBD.B has done a good job of refocusing its business model in recent years, divesting from rail transportation and concentrating on small aircraft manufacturing. This shift has seen the company streamline operations, improve financial health, and enhance profitability.

The stock has ripped higher since my analysis late last year, up 30% versus a 7.5% jump in the S&P 500. Prompting another price re-rating higher, BBD.B just announced one of the largest firm orders in its history – 50 business jets from an undisclosed customer valued at $1.7Bn, sending the stock soaring 21% on July 2. The deal is valued at up to $4Bn based on list prices if the full option is exercised for 120 jets. While the exact models were not disclosed, the company noted that the order is consistent with the customer's existing fleet, suggesting a likely inclusion of Global 7500 aircraft. This order lifts BBD.B’s already healthy backlog by at least 12% and reflects strong demand from operators seeking longer-range, high-performance jets.

BBD.B has made some good progress over the past year in boosting its balance sheet and improving operations, yet certain risks linger. The current macroeconomic landscape presents challenges, including fluctuating steel and aluminum prices/tariffs, which could adversely affect customer demand. However, BBD.B's strategic efforts to broaden its global reach, alongside a focus on aftermarket services and defense, position the company favorably for continued growth. That said, I continue to rate BBD.B as a Buy, with a price target of $158, derived from a 9x EV/EBITDA multiple for 2025, in line with industry peers, if still conservative, and I expect this deal reinforces BBD.B's resurgence.

Updated Deal

The recent 50-aircraft order strengthens BBD.B’s positioning in the large fleet and fractional ownership market.
